coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based “Turn-On” nanofluorescence sensor using a nitrogen-doped carbon dot-hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet architecture and application to α-glucosidase inhibitor screening
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guoliang
Kong, Weiheng
Zhao, Mei
Lu, Shuaimin
Gong, Peiwei
Chen, Guang
Xia, Lian
Wang, Hua
You, Jinmao
Wu, Yongning
coredata.dc:description: The medicines targeted at α-glucosidase played an important role in anti-diabetes and anti-HIV therapy. Unfortunately, the method based on fluorescent assay strategy for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening remains poorly investigated. In this study, a novel “Turn On” fluorescence sensor platform has been developed for trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening from natural medicines. Firstly, carbon dots were prepared by one-pot synthesis and used as the signal output. Combining with the carbon dots, cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes were employed to build the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensor platform. Secondly, L-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl (AAG) was innovatively introduced as α-glucosidase substrate. With hydrolysis of AAG by α-glucosidase, ascorbic acids (AA) were released that can rapidly reduce CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+, and then FRET was stopped accompanying with the fluorescence recovery of CDs. The sensor platform was ultrasensitive to AA with a detection limit of 5nM, ensuring the sensitive monitoring of enzyme activity. Acarbose was used as the inhibitor model and its inhibition rate is proportional to the logarithm of concentration in range of 10−9–10−3 M with the correlation coefficient of R 2=0.996, and an ultralow limit of detection of ~1×10−9 M was obtained. The inhibiting ability of seven compounds isolated from natural medicines was also evaluated. The constructed sensor platform was proven to be sensitive and selective as well as cost-effective, facile and reliable, making it promising as a candidate for trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
α-Glucosidase
Enzyme inhibition
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CoOOH nanosheets and FRET-based CDs- CoOOH sensing probe
Trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening from natural product
Characterization of N-doped CDs and N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor
Investigation of FRET based N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensing mechanism
Ultrasensitive screening of inhibitors from natural products
The selectivity of the proposed method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Processing and Safety
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31301595 , No. 21275089 and No. 21475074 ), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Processing and Safety ( GDPKLAPPS1401 ) and PhD research start-up funds of Qufu Normal University ( bsqd 2012017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-doped carbon dots (ⅰ) and FRET-based sensing platform (ⅱ), hydrolysis of AAG by α-glucosidase to stop FRET and yields of fluorescent signal readout (ⅲ) and application of the proposed sensing platform to screen α-glucosidase inhibitor from natural products (ⅳ).
TEM and HRTEM images of the as-prepared N-doped CDs (a) and N-doped CDs-CoOOH probe (b), survey XPS spectra of N-doped CDs (c–e), and FT-IR spectra of N-doped CDs powder (f).

The reaction scheme of AA with CoOOH (a), and mass spectrometric data of reaction product (MS and MS/MS) and cleavage mode (b and c); mass spectrometer conditions: APCI in positive ion detection mode; nebulizer pressure 60psi; dry gas temperature, 350°C; dry gas flow, 5.0L/min. APCI Vap temperature 350°C; corona current 4000nA; capillary voltage 3500V.

Fluorescence intensity of CDs affected by different CoOOH concentrations from 0 to 0.6mM (a) and the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus concentrations of CoOOH nanosheets (from 0.1 to 0.6mM). The inset photographs of the CDs solution was obtained with a UV lamp (365nm).

Optimization of α-glucosidase activity assay including pH of PBS solutions (pH=6.3, 6.6, 6.9 and 7.2) (a), incubation temperatures (27°C, 37°C, 47°C and 57°C) (b) and enzyme reaction times ( from 5 to 30min) (c).

Investigation of α-glucosidase inhibitor from natural medicines: fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs-CoOOH system in the presence of various concentrations of acarbose (a1), oleanolic acid (b1) and maslinic acid (c1), and their calibration plots (a2, b2 and c2). The inhibitors were incubated with 0.1U/mL enzymes and 0.01M AAG. Fluorescence spectrum was measured with the excitation of 405nm.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based “Turn-On” nanofluorescence sensor using a nitrogen-doped carbon dot-hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet architecture and application to α-glucosidase inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratories of Chemical Safety and Health, China National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment, Beijing 100050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratories of Chemical Safety and Health, China National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The medicines targeted at α-glucosidase played an important role in anti-diabetes and anti-HIV therapy. Unfortunately, the method based on fluorescent assay strategy for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening remains poorly investigated. In this study, a novel “Turn On” fluorescence sensor platform has been developed for trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening from natural medicines. Firstly, carbon dots were prepared by one-pot synthesis and used as the signal output. Combining with the carbon dots, cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes were employed to build the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensor platform. Secondly, L-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl (AAG) was innovatively introduced as α-glucosidase substrate. With hydrolysis of AAG by α-glucosidase, ascorbic acids (AA) were released that can rapidly reduce CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+, and then FRET was stopped accompanying with the fluorescence recovery of CDs. The sensor platform was ultrasensitive to AA with a detection limit of 5nM, ensuring the sensitive monitoring of enzyme activity. Acarbose was used as the inhibitor model and its inhibition rate is proportional to the logarithm of concentration in range of 10−9–10−3 M with the correlation coefficient of R 2=0.996, and an ultralow limit of detection of ~1×10−9 M was obtained. The inhibiting ability of seven compounds isolated from natural medicines was also evaluated. The constructed sensor platform was proven to be sensitive and selective as well as cost-effective, facile and reliable, making it promising as a candidate for trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is one of the most common chronic diseases in nearly all countries. The diabetes often accompanied with the fearful late complications like neuropathy, microangiopathy, retinopathy and cataract (Perry et al., 2014; Veiseh and Langer, 2015). α-Glucosidase is a membrane-bound enzyme at the epithelium of the small intestine, which hydrolyses the cleavage of glucose from disaccharides and oligosaccharides. α-Glucosidase has become one of the most important targets in diabetes therapy, and the inhibitors of this enzyme can prevent these late complications by suppressing the postprandial rise of blood glucose (Zareen et al., 2008). Moreover, the investigation of α-glucosidase inhibitors is also considered to be important in screening of anti-viral compounds such as anti-HIV (Simões‐Pires et al., 2009). Compared to chemical drugs, the medicines from natural products are receiving more and more attentions in diabetes therapy due to their significant curative effect and less side effect. Unfortunately, lots of facts indicate the higher active compounds often present at trace content in medicinal plants. Moreover, the separation and purification of the active compounds has been a headache for the drug development scholars. Thus, methods for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening should be extremely sensitive and with little sample consumption, which will make the drug discovery more effective.

Nanobiosensor strategies become particular prevailing and deserve more and more attentions due to the intrinsic advantages such as high sensitivity and selectivity, and directly monitoring the target enzymes without a complicated pretreatment (Chen et al., 2015a; Chen et al., 2015b; Hou et al., 2015; Teng et al., 2015). But there is no report about the fluorescent sensing of α-glucosidase activity. Therefore, it still remains a challenge in the field of natural medicine to achieve a sensitive, simple, inexpensive and rapid platform for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening.

The newly emerging photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive research interest. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, CDs possess outstanding properties including excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water solubility, environmental friendliness, easy preparation, and cheap cost (Barati et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015b; Lim et al., 2015; Stanisavljevic et al., 2015; Weng et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2015). In this study, we first reported a simple approach for the preparation of cost effective and high photoluminescent CDs from organic compounds (catechol and ethylenediamine), by one-pot synthesis. It is worthy to note that most reported CDs emit blue light, and there have been significant efforts to shift the maximum emission light, but the obtained CDs usually showed low quantum yields, or their synthesis involving complex procedures and toxic components (Wang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2015). The CDs obtained in this study emitted strong green fluorescence and gave an enhanced quantum yield of 49%, which was much higher than the recently reported CDs (most of them less than 10%, Table S1). Based on the synthesized N-doped CDs, we described the development of a “Turn-On” fluorogenic assay for trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening ( Fig. 1). Firstly, cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes were employed to build a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensing probe (i.e. N-doped CDs-CoOOH probe), in which the CDs were efficiently turned off by CoOOH due to FRET. Secondly, L-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl (AAG) was innovatively introduced as α-glucosidase substrate. The substrate AAG itself has no reducibility, while with the hydrolysis of AAG by α-glucosidase, AA was released that could rapidly reduce CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+, and then the fluorescence of CDs was turned on due to the stop of FRET. In this step, N-doped CDs-CoOOH probe was ultrasensitive to AA, and an exciting limit of detection (5nM) was achieved, which was significantly lower than the reported methods (Deng et al., 2011; Li et al., 2015a; Li et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015), ensuring the sensitive monitoring of α-glucosidase activity. Finally, the constructed sensing probe was employed to screen α-glucosidase inhibitors from natural products. Acarbose as one of α-glucosidase inhibitors was studied as model. The developed detection platform gave a satisfactory detection limit of model. The developed detection platform gave model. The developed detection platform gave model. The developed detection platform gave a satisfactory detection limit of ~1×10−9 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, indicating the ultrasensitivity to α-glucosidase inhibitor. Seven monomers isolated from natural products (i.e. maslinic acid, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, ginsenoside-Rf, betulinic acid, betulonic acid and ganoderic acid A) were screened by the proposed sensing platform, demonstrating the excellent applicability. The constructed sensor platform was proven to be facile and cost-effective as well as highly sensitive, selective and reliable, making it promising as a candidate for trace inhibitor screening from natural products. Furthermore, our study provides a new insight on the detection of targeted enzymes by fluorescence CDs nanosensor and will trigger the broad application of CDs in the development of new biological sensors.

Catechol (97%), acarbose (98%), AAG (98%), ethylenediamine and α-glucosidase were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma-Aldrich Company, USA). Oleanolic acid and maslinic acid were isolated from Punica granatum L and identified by our previous study (Li et al., 2011). All stock solutions were stored at 4°C in the refrigerator. High purity water purified with a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Molsheim, France) was used throughout the experiment. Other reagents used were of analytical reagent grade (Shanghai Chemical Reagents Corp., Shanghai, China).

The fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a Hitachi UV-2910 spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer using KBr pellets. The morphologies and sizes of N-doped CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-600, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a PHI 5702 spectrometer. The mass spectrum identification was performed using an Agilent (Palo Alto, CA) 1100 series LC/MSD SL equipped with an autoinjector, and a mass spectrometer (MSD Trap SL, model G2445D) from Bruker Daltonik (Bremen, Germany).

In brief, 300μL of ethanediamine and 25mL of 0.1M catechol were mixed by ultrasonic and then transferred into a 50mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. The resultant dark brown mixture was diluted by 20mL water, and then was centrifuged to remove the large dots at 12,000rpm for 10min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48h. The as-prepared CDs were diluted by water and stored at 4°C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CoOOH nanosheets and FRET-based CDs- CoOOH sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of CoOOH was carried out according to the reported studies with minor revision (Li et al., 2014, 2015a). In brief, 300μL of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 1M) was added to 1mL of CoCl2 (10mM) solution and then sonicated for 1min. Subsequently, 50μL of sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, 0.9M) was added to the centrifuged mixture and sonicated for 10min, and then was adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding a 1.0M HCl solution with stirring. Finally, the CoOOH nanosheets were prepared well and diluted by water for use.

FRET-based CDs-CoOOH sensing probe were constructed by the following steps: 1mL of the prepared N-doped CDs solution, 100μL of ethylene glycol and 40μL of CoOOH nanosheets were added to a centrifuge tube, and then mixture was sonicated for 10min. After that, FRET-based CDs-CoOOH sensing probe was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trace α-glucosidase inhibitor screening from natural product
ce:section.ce:para: Acarbose as a famous α-glucosidase inhibitor was employed to verify the feasibility of α-glucosidase inhibitor screening platform. The inhibiting activity of seven natural compounds (i.e. maslinic acid, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, ginsenoside-Rf, betulinic acid, betulonic acid and ganoderic acid A) was also screened, and their structures are presented in Fig. S1. Different concentrations of the tested compounds were prepared by dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), respectively. Assays for α-glucosidase inhibitors were as follow: (1) 50μL of α-glucosidase (0.1U/mL) was incubated in Phosphate Buffer (PBS, pH=6.9) containing different doses of inhibitors using 100μL AAG (0.01M) as a substrate at 37°C for 15min, and acetonitrile was added to terminate the reaction, and then the mixture was centrifuged for 2min at 4000rpm; (2) the supernatant was removed and added to the prepared sensing probe, and reacted 6min at room temperature. The mixture was submitted to spectral measurements and the emission spectrum was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-doped CDs and N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of the as-prepared N-doped CDs are showed in Fig. 2a. TEM observation clearly confirmed the CDs were successfully obtained, which displayed a narrow diameter distribution (ca. 5–7nm) and excellent monodispersity in aqueous solution (Fig. 2a). These results indicated that the one pot solid-phase method was efficient for the fabrication of CDs. The TEM image of N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor (Fig. 2b) shows that the morphology of CoOOH nanoflakes is presented in the form of hexagonal, and the acquisition of the CDs-CoOOH composite is observed with TEM images, which clearly shows that the C-dots are well interspersed on the surface of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. 2b). The hexagonal nanoflake would provide a large surface area for reacting with a trace of AA. Furthermore, HRTEM images of N-doped CDs-CoOOH are shown in Fig. S2.

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the as-prepared N-doped CDs were carried out by XPS. The full range XPS analysis (Fig. 2c) of the as-prepared CDs sample shows three peaks at 284.5, 399.9, and 531.5eV, which are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. In the expanded XPS spectra, the C1s peaks at 284.7, 285.9, 287.6, and 288.6eV shown in Fig. 2d can be assigned to carbon in the form of C–C(sp3), C–N (sp3), C=O (sp2) and O–C=O (sp2), respectively (Jiang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). The N 1s peaks at 399.7 and 401.9eV indicated that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of (C) 3–N (sp3) and N–H (sp3), respectively (Fig. 2e) (Jiang et al., 2015). This verified that the synthesized CDs were N-doped CDs. FT-IR spectroscopy was also employed to characterize N-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 2f, the synthesized N-doped CDs shows the main absorption bands of O–H stretching vibration at 3438cm−1 and C=C stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons at 1630cm−1 in the spectrum(Jiang et al., 2015). The characteristic stretch band of the amine III C–N bond is at 1481cm−1. The bending vibration of the C–O bond and the stretching peak of the C–O–C bond appeared at 1400 and 1085cm−1, respectively. The peaks emerged at 1340 and 1229cm−1 can be attributed to aliphatic C-N= and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively (Jiang et al., 2015). From the above results, we can deduce the binding between CDs and CoOOH may be due to the complexation of the hydroxyl groups of CDs with CoOOH (Li et al., 2014; 2015a).

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of the N-doped CDs were also investigated. Strong green photoluminescence in water under the UV lamp can be observed for the N-doped CDs (the inset in Fig. 1), which is obtained under UV light (365nm). The UV–vis spectrum shows a maximum absorption peak at 405nm (Fig. S3), and under this excitation wavelength, there is a strong peak at 510nm. The quantum yield (QY) of N-doped-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as reference (Supporting Information). The QY of the new CDs was 49%, which was significantly higher than the previous CDs (Table S1). These excellent characters make the new CDs to promise as bio-imaging and chemical sensing nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of FRET based N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of FRET was investigated by UV spectrum. As shown in Fig. S3, CoOOH nanoflake exhibits a wide absorption spectrum from 320 to 700nm, which has a good spectrum overlap with the emission of the N-doped CDs (510nm) and promises the generation of FRET (Deng et al., 2011; Li et al., 2014, 2015a). When AA was added into CDs-CoOOH sensor solution, CoOOH nanosheets can be reduced and decomposed. The reaction ratio of AA and CoOOH was 1:2 and the reaction scheme is shown in Fig. 3a. Finally, the FRET process was inhibited and the fluorescence of CDs recovered. In order to prove this scheme, the reactive solution was submitted to HPLC–MS analysis and mass spectrometric data is presented in Fig.3b and 3c. As expected, AA was oxidized by CoOOH, and this reaction produced the new substances of dehydroascorbic acid and Co2+, and their complex exhibited intense quasi-molecular ion peak of [M+H]+ at m/z=232.6 (Fig. 3b). The collision-induced dissociation spectra (MS/MS) produced the fragment ions at m/z 230.5, 214.5, 170.7 and 152.6 (Fig. 3c). The mass spectrometric data further verified the mechanism of the developed N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor. The kinetic behaviors of AA reacting with N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor were also studied by monitoring the fluorescence recovery with the time variation. As seen from Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity increases with the elongation of time and reached equilibrium after 6min, revealing a rapid decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes by AA at room temperature.

Meanwhile, based on the property of CDs-CoOOH sensor, the selection of α-glucosidase substrate was also vital, which should take the following into considerations. On one hand, the structure of the substrate should meet the catalytic character of α-glucosidase, that is, it must possess the component of α-glucoside. On the other hand, the substrate itself should not cause the response of the N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor, while the enzymatic hydrolysate ought to induce the response. In this study, AAG was innovatively introduced to act as the substrate of α-glucosidase. After enzymatic hydrolysis, AA was released from AAG that can rapidly reduce CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+ accompanied with the collapse of CoOOH nanoflakes, which finally lead to the fluorescence recovery. Thus, the enzymatic activity would be directly reflected by the levels of fluorescence recovery.

Concentrations of the donor (i.e. N-doped CDs) and the acceptor (i.e. CoOOH nanoflakes) were optimized to achieve the best sensing performance. Different concentrations of CoOOH were added into 1mL of the prepared CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased significantly with the increase of CoOOH concentration from 0 to 0.4mM, and then reached a plateau even with higher concentrations (0.5 and 0.6mM). The maximum quenching efficiency could reach at 92%. We further investigated the sensitivity of the nanoprobes with different CoOOH concentrations (0.2–0.4mM) to AA, and results are given in Fig. S5. By adding low, medium and high AA concentrations (10nM, 100nM and 500nM) to the N-doped CDs-CoOOH probes, the usage amount of 0.4mM CoOOH yielded the lowest fluorescence recoveries and showed almost no response to the low concentration of AA. In addition, 0.2mM and 0.3mM CoOOH obtained the similar fluorescence recoveries. The above results indicated high concentration of CoOOH can yield better quenching efficiency, but the excess CoOOH may result in CDs-CoOOH probe insensitive to AA. Therefore, in consideration of sensitivity and quenching efficiency, 0.3mM of CoOOH nanoflakes was taken to construct the FRET-based sensing probe. The stability of the sensor was also investigated at different pH values. When the nanoprobe was in different pH conditions, the fluorescence intensity showed almost constant from pH 6.0 to pH 8.0 (Fig. S6). Thus, the favorable neutral condition was selected in the later sensing processes.

Based on the above conditions, the sensitivity of N-doped CDs-CoOOH probe was investigated. The probe revealed a good response to the increased AA concentrations (Fig. S7), and more importantly, the fluorescence enhancement is found to be linearly depending on the concentrations of AA ranging from 0.01 to 100μM with a linear equation of △F (F 1−F 0)=16.76+54.73 LogC (nM) and a good correlation coefficient of 0.997 (Fig. S7). The limit of detection (LOD) for AA was calculated to be 5nM according to the 3σ (signal to noise) criteria. Compared with the recent AA fluorescent probes including FRET-based probes (Deng et al., 2011; Li et al., 2014, 2015a; Zhu et al., 2015), the present N-doped CDs-CoOOH sensor showed higher sensitivity (Table S2). The excellent linear correlation and satisfactory sensitivity ensured the sensitive screening of α-glucosidase inhibitor. Furthermore, this sensor shows great potential for determination of trace AA in biological samples.

The main conditions affecting α-glucosidase activity were optimized such as pH of solution, the reaction temperature and the incubation time. By referring to several reported studies for α-glucosidase assay (Simões‐Pires et al., 2009; Zareen et al., 2008), PBS solutions with different pH (i.e. pH=6.3, 6.6, 6.9 and 7.2) were estimated, and results indicated pH=6.9 gave the best fluorescence recovery, which was selected for the following experiments ( Fig. 5a). The effect of various temperatures (i.e. 27°C, 37°C, 47°C and 57°C) on the fluorescence intensity were investigated (Fig. 5b), and 37°C yielded the highest fluorescence intensity and was used as the reaction temperature. Fig. 5c displays a typical time-dependent fluorescence intensity of CDs-CoOOH sensor after mixed with 0.1U/mL of α-glucosidase in PBS buffer (pH=6.9) at 37°C. As the incubation time was prolonged, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually and up to 15min the longer time would not affect the fluorescence recovery (Fig. 5c). Therefore, 15min was chosen as the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive screening of inhibitors from natural products
ce:section.ce:para:
The established fluorescent screening platform was applied to study the inhibitor of α-glucosidase, and the inhibiting ability can be sensitively reported by the levels of fluorescence recovery. As described in Fig. 1, when α-glucosidase was inhibited, the production of AA would be restricted, and finally the fluorescent recovery was depressed. Acarbose as one of α-glucosidase inhibitor was widely used in clinic. Taking acarbose as a model, the detection of α-glucosidase inhibitor was examined. As shown in Fig. 6a, when the inhibitors are added into the α-glucosidase reaction mixture, the levels of fluorescence recovery decrease relative to the doses of the inhibitors. Serials of acarbose concentrations (5nM, 10nM, 50nM, 100nM, 500nM, 1μM, 10μM, 100μM and 1mM) were tested to build regression equation. The inhibition rate (I%) was used to express the enzyme inhibiting ability. The regression equation was I(%)=
F
I
−
F
0
F
E
−
F
0
×
100
, where F 0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without α-glucosidase, FE stands for the fluorescence intensity after recovering by α-glucosidase without inhibitor, and FI is the fluorescence intensity after the inhibiting effect by the inhibitor. The regression equation was I(%)=116.84+10.68logC (M, R 2=0.996). The developed platform gave a detection limit of ~1×10−9 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, indicating the ultra-sensitivity. By calculating the regression equation, IC50 of acarbose was 55.7μM. In order to further prove the applicability of the developed method, seven compounds belonging to terpenoids (their schemes presented in Fig. S1) were screened. The results of the representative maslinic acid (MA) and oleanolic acid (MA) are shown in Fig. 6b and c. It could be seen that our method can efficiently detect the inhibition ability driven by MA and OA. The good linear correlations of I(%)=115.46+18.73log COA (M, R 2=0.995) and I(%) =119.59+17.88logCMA (M, R 2=0.997) were obtained for OA and MA, respectively, and their limits of detection (LOD) were ~4×10−6 M and ~1×10−6 M, respectively. By calculating the regression equations, IC50 of OA and MA were 33.1mM and 12.8mM, respectively. The IC50 values of acarbose (55.7μM), MA (12.8mM) and OA (33.1mM) indicated that acarbose possessed the most powerful inhibiting activity followed by MA and OA. Because α-glucosidase is an important target in diabetes and HIV therapy, thus these results further verified that the natural medicines containing MA or OA often possess anti-diabetes and anti-HIV activity (Liu, 2005; Mengoni et al., 2002). Moreover, the inhibiting ability of another five natural compounds was also investigated by the developed sensing method and results are shown in Fig. S8. The above results powerfully demonstrate the new sensing platform is capable of screening α-glucosidase inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an important indicator for the proposed method. In order to better simulate the reaction environment, we investigated the fluorescence interference from metal ions as well as others including Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, sodium acetate, sodium citrate, glucose and AAG. It was observed that the tested substances had no effective influence on our detection platform (Fig. S9). The high selectivity of our method is owing to the specificity of α-glucosidase and the specific response of the detection platform.

In this study, new N-doped CDs with the high quantum yield have been prepared by one-pot synthesis. Based on this new CD, a FRET based CDs-CoOOH sensor was developed for highly sensitive and selective screening of α-glucosidase inhibitor. When applied to active compounds screening from natural medicines, it showed excellent applicability and sensitivity. The constructed sensor platform was proven to be facile and cost-effective as well as highly sensitive, selective, and reliable for trace inhibitor screening. In the future, the prepared CDs would be exploited in the fields of bioimaging or constructing other fluorescence detection platforms. Meanwhile, α-glucosidase and its new substrate (AAG) could be employed for signal amplification in biosensor or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31301595, No. 21275089 and No. 21475074), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Aquatic Product Processing and Safety (GDPKLAPPS1401) and PhD research start-up funds of Qufu Normal University (bsqd 2012017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.12.094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hemoglobin detection using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe
